nsf_profile.name: Zoya Popovic

awards.title: Improving Spatial Resolution of Internal Body Thermometry
awards.fund_division: Division of Electrical, Communications and Cyber Systems

fund_divisions: Division of Electrical, Communications and Cyber Systems

papers.title: Repeatability of Internal Body Temperature Measurements via Microwave Radiometry
papers.venue: United States National Committee of URSI National Radio Science Meeting
papers.abstract: Microwave radiometers offer a non-invasive method for measuring internal body temperature, with several applications in the medical field (P. Momenroodaki et al., “Noninvasive Internal Body Temperature Tracking with Near-Field Microwave Radiometry,” IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques, vol. 66, no. 5, pp. 2535–2545, Dec. 2017). The knowledge of internal body temperature can be helpful in detecting cancer, diagnosing heat stroke in athletes and soldiers, as well as monitoring brain temperature during cardiac surgery (J. Lee et al., “A 1.4-GHz GaAs MMIC Radiometer for Noninvasive Internal Body Thermometry,” in IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques, vol. 72, no. 1, pp. 759–770, Jan. 2024). While existing research has demonstrated the potential of microwave radiometry for measuring the temperature of internal tissue layers, there have not been sufficient studies of the reliability across different patients or conditions. This work evaluates the repeatability and limitations of the temperature measurements using a radiometer.

papers.title: S-Band Active Circulator with Reactive Load Tuning for Isolation Recovery
papers.venue: Wireless and Microwave Technology Conference
papers.abstract: This work presents a $3.4-\text{GHz}$ gallium nitride (GaN) active circulator with independently tunable reactive loads, which can compensate for impedance mismatch and maintain isolation that results from phased array scan impedance. Simulated results demonstrate isolation improvements of 13.4, 10.5, 19.7 and 31.8 dB for $|\Gamma|$ mismatches of $0.2\left(\theta=180^{\circ}\right.$ and $\left.\theta=270^{\circ}\right)$ and 0.3 $(\theta=0^{\circ}$ and $\theta=90^{\circ})$, respectively. This concept shows promise for application in full duplex phased array front ends, where high isolation is critical.

papers.title: A 77-GHz Power Amplifier in a 90-nm GaN Process
papers.venue: 2025 IEEE Topical Conference on RF/Microwave Power Amplifiers for Radio and Wireless Applications (PAWR)
papers.abstract: This paper demonstrates a three-stage 77-GHz MMIC PA in a 90-nm GaN process under development. Only a single device-size non-linear model was available and no device scaling allowed. Resulting in a limited staging ratio of 1:1:2, four $\mathbf{8} \times \mathbf{5 0} \mu \mathrm{m}$ HEMTs are used for two drive stages and an output reactively power-combined stage. Small-signal measurements show 7.1 dB of gain, which is about a 4 dB degradation compared to simulations. Due to the drop in gain and limited available power, the peak PAE and power could not be reached in measurement. Two PAs are cascaded by wire-bonds to compensate for limited available drive power. Measured large-signal performance for the cascaded amplifiers at $P_{{in }}=9 \mathbf{d B m}$ shows $P_{{out }}=22.5 \mathbf{d B m}$, a large-signal gain of 12.78 dB and $P A E=2.5 \%$. The chip layout with 6 individual PAs allows for additional off-chip power combining.

papers.title: A 20-32 Ghz Ka-Band MMIC LNA in GaN-on-SiC Designed for a Heterogeneous Integration Process
papers.venue: Wireless and Microwave Technology Conference
papers.abstract: This paper presents a gallium nitride (GaN)-on-SiC Monolithic Microwave Integrated Circuit (MMIC) Low Noise Amplifier (LNA) with high dynamic range and low noise figure in a 150 - nm commercial process for use in a heterogeneous integration package. The two-stage MMIC LNA has a peak linear gain of 15.8 dB and a minimum noise figure of 2.6 dB from 20 to 32 GHz while consuming 300 mW of dc power. The measured 1-dB compression point (P1dB) at 22 GHz is 20 dBm. The effects on simulated performance due to the device selection, matching techniques, and packaging stackup will also be discussed. Finally, we will comment on future plans to test the LNA inside a heterogeneously packaged environment.

papers.title: Multi-Watt GaN MMIC Power Amplifiers for 32 GHz Deep Space Communications and Radio Science
papers.venue: United States National Committee of URSI National Radio Science Meeting
papers.abstract: The RF communications of spacecraft-ground and spacecraft-spacecraft play an essential role in space exploration and space science. Radio science instruments commonly utilize existing RF communications channels to extract scientific information from radio wave propagation (Asmar, Sami, et al. “Convergence of Technologies Enabling Radio Crosslink Missions for Planetary Atmospheric Structures.” 2024 IEEE Aerospace Conference. IEEE, 2024). For future missions into deep space, communications at Ka-band carrier frequencies may significantly increase the data rate and measurement resolution to maximize the scientific return when compared with S and X-bands. The goal of this project is to enhance the efficiency and output power of power amplifiers (PAs) at the MMIC level to improve solid-state transmitters for use in deep space communications. Using advanced fabrication processes including WIN 120nm and Qorvo 150nm GaN on SiC, two Ka-band (32GHz) PAs are designed using different power combining techniques to achieve between 5–10 W of output power.

papers.title: Effects of Weather Events on Wireless Power Receiver Arrays
papers.venue: United States National Committee of URSI National Radio Science Meeting
papers.abstract: This talk describes environmental testing of a 5×5 array of 10-GHz patch antenna rectifier antennas (rectennas), composed of unit cell modules (Montgomery et al., European Microwave Conference, Sept. 2024). These arrays can be scaled to larger sizes, including up to 25×25 elements to form a one meter by one meter array. The main substrate of the array is flexible PTFE foam, chosen for its hydrophobic properties and ability to withstand a wide range of temperatures. For the ground plane, a copper fabric is chosen for mechanical flexibility. The top substrate with metallization for the antennas and circuitry is polyamide with copper cladding, with silicon Schottky diodes soldered to rectify the received RF incident waves and deliver power to a dc load. The motivation for the design of the scalable rectenna arrays is to quickly provide power in energy denied environments regardless of conditions such as weather or time of day. The rectenna array therefore needs to be able to withstand a variety of weather conditions with little to no deterioration in its operation.

papers.title: Mutual Coupling Study of Full-Duplex Arrays
papers.venue: United States National Committee of URSI National Radio Science Meeting
papers.abstract: In-band full-duplex (IBFD) phased arrays not only allow for the concurrent use of frequency spectrum, but can also facilitate applications sharing common hardware for directional radiation. These systems require the suppression of both self- and cross-talk interference, which are both influenced by the mutual coupling between elements within the array. This paper presents a study of mutual coupling for linear arrays with patch elements designed to operate at 3.0 GHz. The differences between E- and H-plane coupling as well as a coupling-reduction technique are discussed and simulation results are presented with the intention that this study can inform the design of scalable IBFD arrays.

papers.title: W-Band Power Amplifier With Reconfigurable Output Matching Network
papers.venue: Wireless and Microwave Technology Conference
papers.abstract: This paper presents an on-chip load-reconfigurable three-stage MMIC power amplifier (PA) in a 40-nm GaN process. A staging ratio of 1: 2: 4 is used for two drive stages and an output reactively power-combined stage. The output part of the matching network following the combiner is a discrete reconfigurable impedance matching network that uses two shunt $4 \times 37.5 \mu\ \mathrm{m}$ HEMTs. The tunability is achieved by changing the gate bias voltage of the devices between 3 states. The reconfigurable network enhances efficiency and output power of the PA as the load impedance is varied from $\vert\Gamma\vert=[0-0.8]$ and $\angle \Gamma$ from $[0-360^{\circ}]$. The PA small-signal measurements show a peak gain of 18.1 dB at 77 GHz. For an input power of 11 dBm, the output power is $25\ \text{dBm}(0.3\ \mathrm{W}$), with a large-signal gain of 13 dB and power-added efficiency of 10 % at 77.2 GHz.

papers.title: A Ku-band GaN PA with GaAs Gate Modulator for Improved Linearity
papers.venue: 2025 International Workshop on Integrated Nonlinear Microwave and Millimetre-Wave Circuits (INMMIC)
papers.abstract: A Ku-Band GaN power amplifier (PA) and a GaAs gate modulator are presented. The MMICs work together to amplify signals efficiently while maintaining linearity via gate modulation. In particular, the GaN PA demonstrates a power added efficiency (PAE) greater than 30 %, more than 2W of output power, and a large-signal gain exceeding 13 dB from 11–16 GHz. The gate modulator is designed to extract envelopes with instantaneous bandwidths up to 2 GHz and is measured with a 20MHz LTE signal as well as 2-tone signals with bandwidths up to 800 MHz. The measured PA data is used in an envelope simulator to show IMD3 better than 35 dBc at $\overline {{{\mathbf{P}}_{{\text{out }}}}} = 30{\text{dBm}}$ with gate modulation for a 100MHz 2-tone signal. This represents a 20 dB improvement in IMD3 compared to the static bias case.

papers.title: High-Frequency Flying Capacitor Four-Level Drain Supply Modulator
papers.venue: Applied Power Electronics Conference
papers.abstract: This paper presents a high-frequency, four-level drain supply modulator (DSM) based on a flying capacitor multilevel (FCML) architecture, designed for envelope tracking in radio-frequency power amplifier applications. Operating from a single dc supply voltage, the proposed FCML-DSM employs a simple state-machine-based active balancing of the flying capacitors and control of the output voltage levels at switching rates up to 25 MHz. A hardware prototype of the FCML-DSM is presented, utilizing an FPGA controller and GaN transistors as switching devices in the power stage. The experimental prototype has an output voltage range from 0 V to 20 V, discretized into equally spaced levels, and achieves an average efficiency of 95.4% for output powers exceeding 10 W while tracking a 35 MHz 16-QAM signal envelope.

papers.title: Improving Robustness for Large-Scale Wireless Power Receiver Arrays
papers.venue: United States National Committee of URSI National Radio Science Meeting
papers.abstract: This talk describes 10-GHz patch antenna rectifier arrays with 25 × 25 elements designed using Schottky diodes on 0.127 mm of polyamide and 2 mm of expanded PTFE (ePTFE) on a copper fabric ground plane. These materials were chosen to optimize easy deployment, low cost and easy storage. For this reason, the chosen substrate is flexible and water resistant, ePTFE is a common material used on gaskets and the rectenna arrays can be rolled up like an exercise mat. For the rectifying element, Skyworks SMS7621-079 Schottky diodes are soldered to the metallization (A. Montgomery, J. Molles, C. Scarborough, and Z. Popovic, “High Efficiency 10-GHz Low-Cost Scalable Rectenna Subarrays,” European Microwave Conf., Sept. 2024).

papers.title: Grounded Coplanar Waveguides for Material Characterization
papers.venue: United States National Committee of URSI National Radio Science Meeting
papers.abstract: When designing microwave frequency sensors mounted on human tissue, electrically accurate models of tissue, i.e. phantoms, are necessary. For applications in measuring internal body temperature, material parameters at 1-1.5 GHz are of particular interest (P. Momenroodaki et al., “Noninvasive Internal Body Temperature Tracking with Near-Field Microwave Radiometry,” IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques, vol. 66, no. 5, pp. 2535–2545, Dec. 2017). Previous research suggests the use of transmission lines to extract the effective permittivity from S-parameters (W. B. Weir, “Automatic measurement of complex dielectric constant and permeability at microwave frequencies,” Proceedings of the IEEE, vol. 62, no. 1, pp. 33–36, Jan. 1974). A grounded coplanar waveguide (GCPW) was chosen for the transmission line due to ease of construction and small form factor. From the measured effective permittivity, the filling factors are necessary to obtain the relative permittivity of the material under test. These filling factors are found using a conformal mapping (J. Hu et al., “A grounded coplanar waveguide technique for microwave measurement of complex permittivity and permeability,” IEEE Transactions on Magnetics, vol. 42, no. 7, pp. 1929–1931, Jul. 2006).

papers.title: RF Interference Cancellation for Microwave Thermometry
papers.venue: Intelligent Memory Systems
papers.abstract: This paper presents a method for cancelling external interference in a passive medical sensor for internal body temperature measurements. In microwave thermometry, a radiometric receiver measures the black-body radiation emitted by tissues inside the body. At room temperature and with a few tens of MHz bandwidth, black body radiation has a power around −100 dBm. With such low signal strengths, any transmitter (intentional or not) in the environment becomes a source of RFI. To reduce error in radiometric measurements, a simple and computationally inexpensive method for interference cancellation based on an auxiliary antenna and receiver is presented. Using this technique, temperature fluctuations due to RFI are significantly improved, which have a standard deviation in the error temperature of 3.5 K and max of 56 K, down to 0.38 K and a max of 2 K.

papers.title: A 44-GHz 4×4 MMIC Butler Matrix
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE International Symposium on Phased Array Systems and Technology (ARRAY)
papers.abstract: A 44 GHz Butler matrix designed and manufactured in an InGaAs MMIC process is presented. A compensated crossover is designed to effectively use the two metal layers available in the process while limiting coupling and mismatch to $\mathbf{- 3 0} \text{dB}$ and $\mathbf{- 2 8} \text{dB}$, respectively. The phase shifts are implemented with multiple coupled-line sections and couplers as tandem couplers. On-wafer measurements on a 2.5 mm by 2.5 mm chip show an insertion loss of 2.4 dB and match better than 16.5 dB with amplitude imbalance and phase error of 1.5 dB and 10.8° at 44 GHz. The multiport measurements are enabled with reconfigurable on-chip terminations at the ports. A narrowband patch antenna array on Rogers RT/Duroid 5880 is designed, and the simulated array patterns using the Butler matrix as a feed network are also shown for both single beam and multi-beam performance.

papers.title: Neural Network Model for Tissue Thickness Estimation
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE First International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Medicine, Health and Care (AIMHC)
papers.abstract: This paper explores the use of optimization methods to estimate thicknesses of subcutaneous tissues using a non-invasive near-field antenna with microwave low-power reftec-tometry from 2-13.5 GHz. The initial simple model assumes a planar tissue stack and is implemented with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network trained on full-wave electromagnetic simulation data. A stack of tissue phantoms for skin, fat and muscle with 2 and 3 layers is used for experimental validation. The application for the work presented in this paper is non-invasive internal body temperature measurement using microwave radiometry, where knowledge of tissue thicknesses and electrical parameters is required to solve the inverse problem. These parameters vary based on body composition and type, as well as the specific placement of the sensor on the body. For example, the outermost layer of the forearm epidermis varies by 4.9 mm and upper abdominal fat can vary by 15 mm between people. The model developed for this paper predicts thicknesses of a stackup of Playdough, Rogers Duroid 6010 and a commercial skin phantom within 7 %, 42 %, and 24 % respectively, with a neural network loss value of 0.29. This preliminary model demonstrates the prediction of thicknesses of a more general N-Iayer stackup and serves as a proof of concept for a higher fidelity model that can be used to additionally predict dispersive permittivity and conductivity of layered tissues.

papers.title: Neural Network Model for Breast Tissue Thickness Estimation
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
papers.abstract: This paper explores the use of optimization methods to estimate thicknesses of subcutaneous tissues using a non-invasive near-field antenna with microwave low-power reflectometry from 2-13.5 GHz. An application is in breast cancer detection at microwave frequencies. The initial model assumes a planar tissue stack and is implemented with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network trained on full-wave electromagnetic simulation data. Tissue phantoms for skin, fat and muscle with 2 and 3 layers are used for experimental validation. The model developed for this paper predicts thicknesses of a stackup of Playdough, Rogers Duroid 6010 and a commercial skin phantom within 7%, 41%, and 24% respectively, with a neural network loss value of 0.29. This preliminary model is a proof of concept for a higher fidelity model that can additionally predict dispersive permittivities and conductivities of layered tissues.

papers.title: On-Chip Radiometer with Miniaturized Near-Field Antenna for Internal Body Thermometry
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
papers.abstract: An on-chip correlation-Dicke hybrid radiometer for internal body thermometry at a frequency of 1.4 GHz is presented. The 3.8mm by 2.3mm GaAs MMIC includes two 90° hybrid couplers, a single-stage LNA in each path, and a switch. The hybrid coupler employs lumped elements instead of transmission lines to reduce chip size. Two single-stage LNAs are between the hybrid couplers to enhance robustness against input impedance mismatch. The circuit shows a gain of 13.4 dB, isolation of 16 dB, and a noise figure of 1.31 dB. Following the switch, an off-the-shelf band-pass filter, an on-chip LNA, and a detector are connected to provide a dc output proportional to the temperature at the antenna port. A 3.5 cm diameter circular near-field antenna is designed, capturing thermal noise from the liquid inside a human mouth. The dc voltage at the output is used to compute the temperature detected by the near-field antenna. In-vivo measurements are conducted using orange juice inside the human cheek, showcasing the ability to non-invasively track internal temperature.

papers.title: Integrated 75-100 GHz In-Band Full-Duplex Front End GaN MMIC
papers.venue: Intelligent Memory Systems
papers.abstract: This paper presents a fully integrated 75-100 GHz in-band full-duplex (IBFD) front end MMIC. The MMIC is implemented in a 40-nm HEMT GaN-on-SiC process and consists of a tunable analog self-interference cancellation (SIC) network, power amplifier (PA), and low noise amplifier (LNA). The tunable SIC network, made of three Lange couplers, is connected directly to the antenna port. With tunable loads at the isolated port for two of the couplers, this analog network demonstrates a SIC of 30 dB from transmit to receive based on tunable load biasing. The analog SIC network is then integrated on chip with a three-stage PA and a three-stage LNA, demonstrating a system transmit gain of 13.6 dB and a receive gain of 17 dB.

papers.title: In-Band Full-Duplex Front-End GaN MMIC for Phased Arrays
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE International Symposium on Phased Array Systems and Technology (ARRAY)
papers.abstract: Phased array systems with in-band full-duplex (IBFD) technology can facilitate multifunction operation, such as integrated sensing and communications. Effective designs, however, require unique front-end circuits that can suppress the resulting self-interference and allow array scaling in both size and frequency. This paper presents the design of an IBFD front-end that integrates a power amplifier, low-noise amplifier, tunable canceller, and an antenna interface for element-level IBFD arrays. The circuit is implemented in the WIN Semiconductors NP12 0.12μm GaN process, and is designed with amplifiers that have a small-signal gain higher than 10 dB and an analog canceller with more than 40 dB of transmit-receive isolation enhancement at 3GHz.

papers.title: Tunable Sensitivity Enhancement of a Rydberg-Atom Electrometer
papers.venue: Conference on Precision Electromagnetic Measurements
papers.abstract: This paper presents a metallic resonant cavity for sensitivity enhancement of a Rydberg atom microwave electrometer. The Rubidium-filled vapor cell is encased by the resonator, with appropriate coupling structures for laser beams and the incident radio-frequency field. The resonator is designed to enhance the sensitivity of Rydberg states from 10.19 to 10.77GHz with 620MHz of frequency tunability. The resonator exhibits an intrinsic Q-factor ranging from 4546 to 5520, for ultimate field enhancement factors ranging from 3.5 to 9.3.

papers.title: Field Correlation Radiometry for Improved Spatial Focusing in Internal Temperature Sensing
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
papers.abstract: Microwave radiometry has been demonstrated as a viable method for non-invasive measurements of internal body temperature, including that of the brain. Existing approaches use a single near-field antenna placed on the skin at low microwave frequencies where the sensing depth is on the order of centimeters. Such a near-field antenna integrates the temperature over a relatively large volume. Here we introduce a generalized theory for near field correlation radiometry of multiple radiometers for improving spatial resolution. Results of a numerical study for measuring core brain temperature are shown using two 1.5 GHz near-field antennas placed on the head, with a comparison to the single-antenna case.

papers.title: Antipodal Vivaldi Array for Reconfigurable Aperture-Level In-Band Full-Duplex
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE International Symposium on Phased Array Systems and Technology (ARRAY)
papers.abstract: This paper presents an 8-element linear Antipodal Vivaldi antenna array from 8 to 18 G Hz designed for aperture-level in-band full-duplex operation. The array is designed to be manufactured from a single substrate, with separate feed lines at each element to allow for a completely reconfigurable system. At 10 GHz the antenna element has a width of $3\lambda/4$, corresponding to the spacing between elements, allowing for scanning up to 20°. For $\vert S_{11}\vert < -10\mathbf{dB}$ or better with a width below $\lambda$ for the antenna element, corrugations are added. With all 8-elements, the simulated array demonstrates a gain of 15 dB to 18 dB, and a front-to-back ratio of 16.5 dB or better.

papers.title: Near-Field Analog Correlation of Thermal Noise Signals at X-Band
papers.venue: European Microwave Conference
papers.abstract: This paper presents an analysis of correlation between two thermal noise signals received in the near field from a lossy uniform material at room temperature. The more general analysis is adapted to a specific case that can be experimentally validated. The proposed experiment is designed from 8–12GHz using open-ended WR90 waveguide probes on a cylinder with a diameter of 15cm filled with saline. Full-wave simulations combined with the analog correlation model show that the measured correlated voltage varies from tenths of a milivolt, to tens of microvolts as the angle of separation between waveguides changes.

papers.title: 40–44 GHz Frequency-Tunable MMIC Butler Matrix
papers.venue: European Microwave Conference
papers.abstract: This paper presents a 4-element 44-GHz Butler matrix implemented in a single GaAs chip. The 90° 3-dB couplers are implemented with two coupled line sections, and the tunable phase shifters allow for frequency tuning from 40–44GHz with a 1-V bias change. The reflective phase shifters are implemented with tunable HEMT reactances on two of the ports. The simulated and measured progressive phase profiles are in good agreement, with less than 6° error for 4 available beams from 40 to 44GHz.

papers.title: High-Efficiency 10-GHz Low-Cost Scalable Rectenna Subarrays
papers.venue: European Microwave Conference
papers.abstract: This paper details the design and characterization of rectenna subarrays for far-field wireless power transfer at 10GHz. The designs are scalable to larger areas with dc combining. Two 4-element subarrays are presented: one with a rectifier per antenna element, and one with 4 antenna elements per rectifier. The first subarray consists of dual polarized Coptic-cross elements, optimized for higher incident power densities with dc combining. The second subarray is a linearly polarized patch array with a differential feed coupled to a single active rectifier for improved efficiency at lower incident power densities through RF combining. Both designs are fabricated on a thin layer of polyimide mounted atop expanded PTFE for moisture resistance and mechanical flexibility. For a broadside incident plane wave, the subarrays demonstrate up to 35% conversion efficiency at power densities below 1mW/cm2. An efficiency of 50% is measured for the RF-combined subarray at 0.837mW/cm2 power density.

papers.title: 6-12 GHz Transmit Phased Array Efficiency with Stepped Static Supply Modulation for Beam Shaping
